annotations.label: Bayesian inference

anchor.snippet: is a method of statistical inference in which Bayes' theorem is used to calculate a probability of a hypothesis

annotations.note: Bayesian inference as a 'method' is not a single declaration, but its core operation—the posterior of a likelihood kernel given a prior measure—is formalized as `posterior`.

annotations.label: Bayes' theorem (posterior computation)

anchor.snippet: Bayesian inference computes the posterior probability according to Bayes' theorem

annotations.note: `cond_eq_inv_mul_cond_mul` is explicitly labelled **Bayes' Theorem** and states `μ[t | s] = (μ s)⁻¹ * μ[s | t] * μ t`.

annotations.label: Hypothesis H

anchor.snippet: stands for any hypothesis whose probability may be affected by data

annotations.note: A 'hypothesis' is just an arbitrary measurable event/set and has no dedicated formal definition.

annotations.label: Prior probability

anchor.snippet: is the estimate of the probability of the hypothesis

annotations.note: The prior is just the measure `μ` (called the 'prior measure' in the posterior framework); it has no standalone definition.

annotations.label: Evidence

anchor.snippet: corresponds to new data that were not used in computing the prior probability

annotations.note: Evidence/data is modelled as a point or measurable event with no dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Posterior probability

anchor.snippet: This is what we want to know: the probability of a hypothesis given the observed evidence

annotations.note: The posterior probability of a hypothesis given evidence is the conditional probability `cond` (`μ[H | E]`).

annotations.label: Likelihood

anchor.snippet: it indicates the compatibility of the evidence with the given hypothesis

annotations.note: The likelihood `P(E | H)` is an instance of conditional probability `cond` (and the data-generating kernel `κ` in the posterior framework), but 'likelihood' is not a named concept.

annotations.label: Marginal likelihood (model evidence)

anchor.snippet: This factor is the same for all possible hypotheses being considered

annotations.note: `cond_add_cond_compl_eq` (law of total probability) computes the marginal `μ t` as a weighted sum over hypotheses, but there is no named 'marginal likelihood'.

annotations.label: Posterior proportional to prior times likelihood

anchor.snippet: the posterior probability of a hypothesis is proportional to its prior probability

annotations.note: `posterior_eq_withDensity`, annotated 'a form of Bayes' theorem', writes the posterior as the prior weighted (via `withDensity`) by the likelihood density.

annotations.label: Bayes' rule in odds form

anchor.snippet: Bayes' rule can be rewritten as follows

annotations.note: No odds-form / posterior-odds = prior-odds × likelihood-ratio statement was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rule of multiplication form

anchor.snippet: One quick and easy way to remember the equation would be to use rule of multiplication

annotations.note: `cond_mul_eq_inter` states `μ[t | s] * μ s = μ (s ∩ t)`, the multiplication rule `P(A∩B)=P(B|A)P(A)`.

annotations.label: Jeffrey's rule (probability kinematics)

anchor.snippet: which applies Bayes' rule to the case where the evidence itself is assigned a probability

annotations.note: Jeffrey conditioning / probability kinematics is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General formulation of Bayesian updating

anchor.snippet: Suppose a process is generating independent and identically distributed events

annotations.note: The general measure-theoretic Bayesian update is captured by the posterior kernel and `posterior_eq_withDensity`, though not stated in the article's iid-process form.

annotations.label: Update for multiple iid observations

anchor.snippet: it can be shown by induction that repeated application of the above is equivalent to

annotations.note: The result that repeated Bayesian updating equals a single update with the product likelihood is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: it can be shown by induction that repeated application of the above is equivalent to

annotations.label: Parametric posterior update

anchor.section: Parametric formulation: motivating the formal description
anchor.snippet: By parameterizing the space of models, the belief in all models may be updated in a single step

annotations.note: The parametric posterior over a model space is exactly the `posterior` kernel, characterized by `compProd_posterior_eq_map_swap`.

annotations.label: Data point

anchor.snippet: a data point in general. This may be a vector of values

annotations.note: A data point is just an element of the data space and has no dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Parameter

anchor.snippet: the parameter of the data point's distribution

annotations.note: The parameter is just an element of the parameter space `Ω` in the posterior framework, with no standalone definition.

annotations.label: Hyperparameter

anchor.snippet: the hyperparameter of the parameter distribution

annotations.note: Hyperparameters of a prior distribution are not formalized as a concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sample

anchor.snippet: is the sample, a set of

annotations.note: A 'sample' of n data points has no dedicated definition beyond generic finite tuples/product spaces.

annotations.label: New data point

anchor.snippet: a new data point whose distribution is to be predicted

annotations.note: A new (to-be-predicted) data point is just an element of the data space with no formal definition.

annotations.label: Prior distribution

anchor.snippet: The prior distribution is the distribution of the parameter(s) before any data is observed

annotations.note: The prior distribution is just the 'prior measure' `μ` on the parameter space in the posterior framework; it is not a separate definition.

annotations.label: Sampling distribution (likelihood)

anchor.snippet: The sampling distribution is the distribution of the observed data conditional on its parameters

annotations.note: The sampling distribution is modelled as the data-generating Markov kernel `κ : Kernel Ω 𝓧`, but it is not separately defined as 'sampling distribution'.

annotations.label: Marginal likelihood

anchor.snippet: is the distribution of the observed data marginalized over the parameter(s)

annotations.note: The marginal of data over the parameter is `κ ∘ₘ μ` (`Measure.bind`), but there is no named 'marginal likelihood' declaration.

anchors.snippet: is the distribution of the observed data marginalized over the parameter(s)

annotations.label: Posterior distribution

anchor.snippet: The posterior distribution is the distribution of the parameter(s) after taking into account the observed data

annotations.note: `posterior κ μ` (`κ†μ`) is exactly the posterior distribution of the parameter given the data.

annotations.label: Bayes' rule (posterior ∝ likelihood × prior)

anchor.snippet: posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior

annotations.note: `posterior_eq_withDensity` is documented as 'a form of Bayes' theorem' and expresses the posterior as the prior times the likelihood (Radon–Nikodym) density.

annotations.label: Existence/uniqueness via Radon–Nikodym

anchor.snippet: Existence and uniqueness of the needed conditional expectation is a consequence of the Radon–Nikodym theorem

annotations.note: The Radon–Nikodym theorem itself is formalized as `absolutelyContinuous_iff_withDensity_rnDeriv_eq` (and `withDensity_rnDeriv_eq`).

anchors.snippet: Existence and uniqueness of the needed conditional expectation is a consequence of the Radon–Nikodym theorem

annotations.label: Generalization to improper priors

anchor.snippet: Bayes' theorem can be generalized to include improper prior distributions

annotations.note: The posterior framework requires `IsFiniteMeasure μ`, so the extension to improper (non-finite) priors is not covered.

anchors.snippet: Bayes' theorem can be generalized to include improper prior distributions

annotations.label: Posterior predictive distribution

anchor.snippet: The posterior predictive distribution is the distribution of a new data point, marginalized over the posterior

annotations.note: The posterior predictive is expressible as the likelihood kernel composed with the posterior (`κ ∘ₘ κ†μ`), but is not a named definition.

anchors.snippet: The posterior predictive distribution is the distribution of a new data point, marginalized over the posterior

annotations.label: Prior predictive distribution

anchor.snippet: The prior predictive distribution is the distribution of a new data point, marginalized over the prior

annotations.note: The prior predictive is the marginal `κ ∘ₘ μ` (`Measure.bind`), present as a construction but not under that name.

anchors.snippet: The prior predictive distribution is the distribution of a new data point, marginalized over the prior

annotations.label: Predictive distributions are compound distributions

anchor.snippet: Both types of predictive distributions have the form of a compound probability distribution

annotations.note: Compound/mixture distributions are exactly `Measure.bind` (`κ ∘ₘ μ`), but the predictive-equals-compound statement is not stated as a result.

annotations.label: Interpretation of evidence factor

anchor.snippet: if the model were true, the evidence would be more likely than is predicted by the current state of belief

annotations.note: This is interpretive commentary about the support factor, not a formal statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cromwell's rule

anchor.snippet: This can be interpreted to mean that hard convictions are insensitive to counter-evidence

annotations.note: Cromwell's rule (priors of 0 or 1 cannot be updated) is not formalized as such in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bernstein–von Mises theorem

anchor.snippet: in the limit of infinite trials, the posterior converges to a Gaussian distribution

annotations.note: The Bernstein–von Mises theorem (asymptotic normality of the posterior) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conjugate prior

anchor.snippet: the corresponding posterior distribution will be in the same family

annotations.note: No notion of conjugate priors / conjugate distribution families exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Posterior median as robust estimator

anchor.snippet: The posterior median is attractive as a robust estimator

annotations.note: The posterior median and its robustness as an estimator are not formalized.

annotations.label: Posterior mean estimator

anchor.snippet: then the posterior mean is a method of estimation

annotations.note: The posterior mean is the Bayes estimator under squared loss; the Bayes-risk decision framework exists but the posterior-mean estimator itself is not defined.

anchors.snippet: then the posterior mean is a method of estimation

annotations.label: Maximum a posteriori estimation (MAP)

anchor.snippet: Taking a value with the greatest probability defines the maximum a posteriori estimation

annotations.note: MAP estimation (argmax of the posterior) is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Taking a value with the greatest probability defines the maximum a posteriori estimation

annotations.label: No MAP estimate attained

anchor.snippet: There are examples where no maximum is attained, in which case the set of MAP estimates is empty

annotations.note: There is no MAP definition in Mathlib, hence no formalized example of a non-attained MAP estimate.

annotations.label: Posterior-risk-minimizing estimators

anchor.snippet: There are other methods of estimation that minimize the posterior risk

annotations.note: `bayesRisk` is the infimum of average risk over estimators, formalizing risk minimization, but no `bayesEstimator` achieving the minimum is defined.

annotations.label: Posterior predictive of a new observation

anchor.snippet: The posterior predictive distribution of a new observation

annotations.note: Expressible as the likelihood kernel composed with the posterior (`Measure.bind`), but the posterior predictive of a new observation is not a named construction.

annotations.label: Cookie bowls problem

anchor.snippet: Suppose there are two full bowls of cookies

annotations.note: This pedagogical worked example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Archaeological pottery dating

anchor.snippet: An archaeologist is working at a site thought to be from the medieval period

annotations.note: This applied worked example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wald's complete class theorem

anchor.section: In frequentist statistics and decision theory
anchor.snippet: who proved that every unique Bayesian procedure is admissible

annotations.note: Admissibility and complete-class theorems are not formalized; the decision framework has only `bayesRisk`/`minimaxRisk`, not Wald's theorem.

annotations.label: Bayes factor in model comparison

anchor.snippet: the ratio of their posterior probabilities corresponds to the Bayes factor

annotations.note: The Bayes factor (ratio of marginal likelihoods) is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Solomonoff's universal prior

anchor.snippet: is the sum of the probabilities of all programs

annotations.note: Algorithmic probability / Solomonoff's universal prior (and Kolmogorov complexity) are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gardner-Medwin courtroom implication

anchor.snippet: A and not- B implies the truth of C , but the reverse is not true

annotations.note: This is an informal courtroom logical illustration, not a formalized statement.
